comments.content:
[IMAGE:0]Midnight is just a few minutes away, and so it's time to launch a new Magic Monday. Ask me anything about occultism, and with certain exceptions, any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. Please note:  Any question or comment received after then will not get an answer, and in fact will just be deleted.  If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.1 of The Magic Monday FAQ here. 

Also: I will not be putting through or answering any more questions about practicing magic around children. I've answered those in simple declarative sentences in the FAQ. If you read the FAQ and don't think your question has been answered, read it again. If that doesn't help, consider remedial reading classes; yes, it really is as simple and straightforward as the FAQ says.  And further:  I've decided that questions involving the evocation of spirits are permanently off topic here. The point of occultism is to develop your own capacities, not to try to bully or wheedle other beings into doing things for you. I've discussed this in a post on my blog.

The image? I field a lot of questions about my books these days, so I've decided to do little capsule summaries of them here, one per week. The book above was my thirty-sixth published book, and to my mind it's the best of the books that came out of my decade of blogging on peak oil. I decided in 2013 that it was time to tackle belief in progress, the secular religion that dominates the modern Western world, and talk about where that ersatz faith came from, what role it plays in our problems, and why it's collapsing around us. That's what After Progress accomplished, and I think it did so very well, covering ground that extended from ancient mythology to the philosophy of Friedrich Nietzsche. Inevitably--the books of mine that I consider my best work always sell very poorly--it was the least popular of my peak oil books, and went out of print sooner than any of the others. It's currently available only used or in ebook form, though that should change next year.

Buy Me A Coffee

Ko-Fi

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use either of the links above to access my online tip jar; Buymeacoffee is good for small tips, Ko-Fi is better for larger ones. (I used to use PayPal but they developed an allergy to free speech, so I've developed an allergy to them.) If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here. 
 
[IMAGE:1]I've also had quite a few people over the years ask me where they should buy my books, and here's the answer. Bookshop.org is an alternative online bookstore that supports local bookstores and authors, which a certain gargantuan corporation doesn't, and I have a shop there, which you can check out here. Please consider patronizing it if you'd like to purchase any of my books online.

And don't forget to look up your Pangalactic New Age Soul Signature at CosmicOom.com.

With that said, have at it!

***This Magic Monday is now closed and no more comments will be put through. See you next week!***

comments.title: Social Technology as a Metaphor and Question on Why Invoking the Valar Only Gets Moderate Results
comments.content:
Good Evening,

May all here who welcome it have a lovely week!

For what it's worth, After Progress is likely my second favorite of your non-practice-focused books (after A World Full of Gods), but it certainly helped that I had been primed by a lot of experiences and reading of your other material to take seriously. If I had encountered it a few years prior, I don't know what I would have made of it.

To Share: In the comments to a recent post by another commenter here, I happened to use the term "social technology" and we had a bit of interesting discussion about it that I decided to expand into a piece talking about its usefulness (or lack thereof) as a metaphor: https://jpowellrussell.com/#on_the_usefulness_of_social_technology_as_a_metaphor

To Ask: Forgive me if this is a repeat, but I can't seem to find more than bits here and there when I search here or the main site. You've said before that you've met folks who invoke the Valar for magical/religious work and get results, but that those results have been not-so-strong. I believe somewhere you said you suspected that Tolkien, as a devout Catholic, might have somehow purposely monkey-wrenched the Valar as suitable targets of worship or invocation.

1. So, first, have I fairly summarized your thinking on the matter, or am I mangling it?
2. If that's roughly right, would you mind expanding a bit on what's going on here and what Tolkien may or may not have done to steer it this way?

For what it's worth, building on what you said in some of the comments I did find, my rough working hypothesis is this: the Valar are a set of astral constructs that have received enough shared attention, emotion, and effort to become somewhat suitable astral vehicles for spirits, and that some spirits (here used in its broadest meaning of "any non-material conscious being") may show up when those vehicles are consciously invoked, but somehow whatever spirits answer the call don't pack as much "oomph" as when Gods from other pantheons are called upon (whether because the vehicles in question are somehow unsuited to more powerful spirits/Gods, or only the less powerful ones are attracted, or whatever other reason).

As always, thanks very much to JMG and everyone else here for all that you do.

My blessings to all who welcome them,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Social Technology as a Metaphor and Question on Why Invoking the Valar Only Gets Moderate Result
replies.content:
You might be amused to know that A World Full of Gods is another of the books I consider my best work, and its sales have been correspondingly modest. For some reason, Heathens pretty consistently seem to like it, and most other people give it a blank look and walk on by.

With regard to the Valar, that's basically correct. I know of several groups of people back in the heyday of the Neopagan scene who tried to launch a cult of the Valar, with all the usual bells and whistles, and none of them got far or accomplished much. That's not just because they're fictional deities, of course -- the equally fictional deities of the Cthulhu mythos have attracted quite a few serious practitioners, who apparently get good results from invoking them, and I know Druids who invoked the completely made-up deity Dalon ap Landu, god of the groves -- the chief deity of the Reformed Druids of North America, which started life as a put-on -- and gotten a definite response.

I've wondered aloud from time to time if Tolkien figured out some way to gimmick the Valar to make them unresponsive to pagan worship, but I'm far from sure that's correct. What I know is that nobody seems to be able to get much of a response by using those names and images.

replies.title: Re: Social Technology as a Metaphor and Question on Why Invoking the Valar Only Gets Moderate Result
replies.content:
Well, that's certainly interesting. My interest in the question has to do with an idea that I've had for a while. Namely, that the gods are communicating through various elements of popular culture, folktales, etc., and that some of these might well eventually form the basis for future American myths and deities. Whether that'll be a Batman-like god or not, I do not know. But I could easily enough imagine a future, many generations from now, where gods like "Washington" and "Lincoln" are worshiped alongside Thor and Odin and Christ.

I can also imagine that some of our fictional heroes might well join those ranks, or gods that have answered to those names, that is. Part of this occurred to me as I was participating in several "Chuck Norris memes" groups on social media. These kind of jokes have been around for a while now, and a couple of things occurred to me about them. In these particular memes, he has taken on powers and abilities that are clearly divine. In addition to that, I quickly noted that many of the participants were actually treating him (or more accurately, this fictional version of him) as an almost folk deity.

That is, many of them seem quite serious at this point. It then occurred to me that he might well be some modern version of Paul Bunyan, and for all I know, a new addition to the American pantheon of divine and semi-divine heroes is being made. All very speculative, and perhaps not something to be taken too seriously. But I can't help sensing something deeper behind a lot of the current fictional, quasi-fictional, and real world heroes that will last long after our civilization crumbles.

So I wouldn't be surprised to see figures from them (or from comic books that were eventually movified) join the pantheon. Exactly which ones will, I don't know. My money would be on Superman and Batman at least; they've stayed popular for an appreciable fraction of a century now, but it depends.

replies.title: Re: Social Technology as a Metaphor and Question on Why Invoking the Valar Only Gets Moderate Result
replies.content:
This is a topic I have been pondering for some time and while my ideas are yet untested hypothesis, please let me share...

I do not think the Valar are actual angels or archangels. I have them pegged as belonging to the "Powers and Principalities" spoken about in Ephesians 6:12. Contrary to what St. Paul seems to imply, I do not think all of them are demonic, or even inimical to human existence. The subset of those type of quasi-angelic beings (Glories is the generic term used by Ft. Fortea) we could call Valar, as probably conceived by a devout Catholic mind as Tolkien's, would be similar in power and dignity to the Olympic Gods (with the exception of Zeus/Jupiter).

In the category I am following, Principalities are beings that are concerned with the destiny of whole nations, not individual humans, just as the beekeeper has a relationship with the whole beehive system, but little or no concern with the fate of individual beings. So, while a group of people would be able to receive blessings from trying to interact with one of those beings, to form a personal connection with one of those would be not on the table. Powers are greater and mightier than Principalities, and also more far removed from human lives; those would be more concerned with fundamental forces of Nature. As such it is good and proper to revere them, but I do not think they would be accessible at all without some form of intermediation.

The Maiar, angels and archangels in orthodox Roman Catholic parlance, are the proper beings whom we should be trying to make contact. IMHO, they would see humans as pets or cattle, respectively. From a literary perspective solely, I'd peg Gandalf as an archangel who has a more personal relationship with Named Characters such as Aragorn and Theoden. The nature of such relationships would be Shepard to Shepard-dogs, the later making it easier for the former to relate with the mass of non individuated Sheep.

Please let me know if this makes any sense, and if this would help explain the lack of results in past attempts to worship Valar.

comments.title: Esoteric Trumpism
comments.content:
Thanks, brenainn, for reminding us last week of the book, Esoteric Trumpism. Hoping it might shed light on recent events, I just read it myself and thought I'd be bold to offer some reflections:

  • Von Hoffmeister's arguments might be too subtle for me, but I found the book frustratingly lightweight. It's a series of loosely-connected micro-essays, all searching for the most apt literary, cinematic or historical analogies for what Trump is doing or embodying. Some passages are written as fiction or prose-poetry and feel very forced to me.
  • Lovecraft and Spengler are touchstones for Von Hoffmeister-- it feels ecosophian that way, but none of the images or ideas are developed with any depth. I haven't read Miguel Serrano, but that author is not named here, and I don't have the sense that this is intended as an engagement with "Esoteric Hitlerism."
  • Frankly, I don't see anything esoteric in the book at all. We're told at one point, "Vril is the universal energy that that flows through and encircles the globe just below its crust," but that paragraph is describing the comic book character Magneto, so I have to presume that's a fiction? comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hello JMG and all.

1. In past life regression books, the subjects report a sort of strangeness with the death state. When the soul takes off, it’s not instant heaven nor hell but rather incredible strangeness and confusion of its surroundings and whereabouts. Some take off immediately, aware of their situation, others as mentioned remain incredibly stiff and confused. No physical pain is reported regardless of the mannerism of the death.
In all of these books I have read, there has been no mention of an archangel, angel, deity, or an ascended master meeting the newly released soul. The person is simply greeted by their loved one(s) and taken off.
Any opinion as to why we don't meet such spirits, especially as occultists?

2. What is the difference between an archangel and a deity/god? We have very little lore about the former save for a few passages in the Bible about Michael, Gabriel and Raphael.

3. Can a talisman work remotely from your location to help your target far away from you? Would the procedure be the same?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Because the great powers of the Unseen have many better things to do than take part in welcoming committees for the newly dead. Remember that on average, 166,000 people die every single day. The welcoming committee of loved ones takes care of that job, and if the newly dead soul is of sufficient stature to interact with cosmic powers, that'll happen after they've finished processing their recent life.

2) Depends on what you mean by those words. "Deity" in particular is a very, very vague term -- monotheists use it one way, polytheists in a flurry of other ways. Remember that all human language is a collection of primate hoots and grunts which we use to very roughly categorize the phenomena of an infinitely complex cosmos!

3) Yes, but it won't be anything like as effective. The procedure is the same.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
It depends on which Bible you are reading, actually. "The Bible" is not quite one and the same book for all eras, all lands, and all Christian churches. Its contents differ strikingly from era to era, land to land, and church to church. Medieval Western European Catholic Bibles in Latin before the Council of Trent usually included the book "Fourth Esdras," in which talks the Archangel Uriel figures.

And since, in the Medieval Catholic West, the Biblical archangels were four in number, they could be put into significant correspondence with other "cosmic fours" such as the four points of the compass, the four seasons, the four ages of man, the four elements, the the four humors and temperaments in man, and so forth. These Medieval cosmologies could become quite elaborate.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1. Because, when we die, we are quite disoriented. We're not likely to be able to find deities or angels of any level when we're not even able to find our own navels any longer. Sort of like a newborn baby discovering that it now has a body and has to figure out how to steer the bloody thing, the newly died similarly have to get the hang of disembodiment. Those familiar greeters come find us because they know we'll initially be in a state of shock and confusion, whereas gods and angels keep their distance, so as not to unduly overwhelm us coming right out the gate.

2. Each of us has a guardian angel from whom we are descended and to whom we will return. Ditto the gods. Archangels are their guardian angels from whom they are always descending and to whom they are always returning, being timeless and all. Like the blind men trying to make sense out of an elephant, the Bible accurately describes a tiny aspect of the immensity of archangels. The Raven stories describe a different aspect, and the Pure August Jade Emperor canon describes yet another.

The Bible's insights are particularly bewildering because it continuously vacillates between considering Yahweh to be a god, the only god, and the life force itself. As one god among many, Yahweh naturally has an archangel watching over him. As the only god, Yahweh would have a bunch of archangels watching over him for some unexplained reason. As the life force, Yahweh spins out a bevy of archangels to watch over all the gods, about whom the Bible gets particularly muddled. Every mythology has deep divine revelations, as well as total blind spots.

— Christophe

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
For what it's worth, if you read Dionysius's Celestial Hierarchy along with its source, Proclus's Theology of Plato, the difference is... nothing at all. The choir of archangels, as one of the lower hierarchies of angels, corresponds with one of the lower hierarchies of gods.

From my own perspective, in actual experience, the difference seems to be one of emphasis. If you want to approach the universe as an unfolding of the will of the One or of God (as Proclus is sometimes happy to refer to the One), then you refer to the Power that proceed from the One as "angels," and approach them as enacting the Will of the First God. If, instead, you want to emphasize the plurarity that exists in the Divine, you refer to them as "Gods," and see them as separate individuals with colorful personalities and even more colorful biographies.

Of course, I'm not being entirely honest-- I said "if you want," and that implies that you have a choice in the matter. Actually you don't get very much say in the matter; the Powers themselves decide which approach is right for you, and guide you in that direction.

comments.title: Space and Destiny
comments.content:
Space - Sometimes when I gaze at an object before me I can "see" the space in between. It feels as if the transparent emptiness between me and whatever I look at has an invisible, living radiance, making whatever I am looking at appear more crisp and alive, general awareness is elevated, too. So far it occurs only spontaneously, but being in a meditative mood definitely helps. When I do the SOP while having this kind of vision, it tends to feel much more powerful than an ordinary one. When I compare drawing the symbols in "ordinary space" and "living space", it feels alike to drawing with a pen with the cap put on or removed. Which makes me wonder what the space that surrounds us really is. Thinking of the CosDoc, I would tend to say something like "the living mind of the logos" - but is it really, am I just making things up?

Destiny - Following your reply to my comment on last weeks post on ecosophia I pondered the concepts of "karma" and "destiny" for a while. Up to now it was my view that the karma of the past shapes the future and that "destiny" is an intersection of an individual karma and the karma of a larger system in such a way, that the individual happens to be in a position to help the collective karma to play out. Thinking about what you said and of the few events in my life where I felt (sometimes only after looking back) that there was destiny at work - I wonder if the "pull of the future" is more akin to "perspective" than to "law"? A glimpse up the planes and through time to see what could be and possibly should be?

Thank you, as always!
Nachtgurke

replies.title: Re: Space and Destiny
replies.content:
1) Well, to start with, there's no space around you. The apparent gap between you and that object is full of air -- 16 lbs. of it for every cubic inch. So you may be seeing that. My guess, though, is that you're perceiving the ether.

2) I see destiny as intention -- the intention of the highest, eternal part of yourself, the one part that is always in harmony with the will of the gods. It's a matter of seeking a goal.

replies.title: Re: Space and Destiny
replies.content:
Interestingly, the physicist in me who is well aware of the (physical) content of the space surrounding him is relatively sure that he doesn't see air but that ether is a more likely explanation (fellow physicists, eat that!). Ether might be an explanation because I perceive a "glow" (it's not glowing) around my fingers during the SOP for a while now. It's maybe 1/2 inch thick.

But the "painting in mind of the logos" thing - if everything is a projection of/into the mind of the logos and a change of place is a change of state - isn't there at least some truth to it, although technically it probably is insignificant?

Cheers,
Nachtgurke

replies.title: Re: Space and Destiny
replies.content: Keep experimenting and find out!

replies.title: Re: Space and Destiny
replies.content: A cubic inch has air in it weighing 0.000043lb. The 14.7 pounds number is the weight of the air in the column of atmosphere above each square inch.

replies.content:
I had this hypothesis that the Trisagion prayer gets its power from ritually drawing attention to Christianity's liminal-semi-violation of the taboo against polytheism that Judaism had done all that work to build up, and from a taboo-like intuitive recoil from trying to equate one with three. I had wondered if there might be sustainability issues there, but I never asked, because it always felt like it would be in poor taste somehow.

...Maybe I accidentally noclipped myself into the Backrooms of Abrahamic religion at some point, and now I can't see them in a way that's compatible with conventional respectful speech anymore, the way an ordinary pious believer might. I see things like how Christianity symbolically semi-violated three or arguably all four of Israelite culture's strongest taboos -- against changing the Sabbath, against worship of other gods, against cannibalism, and arguably against adultery if you lump that in symbolically with worship of other gods -- and how this otherwise glaring fact just sort of gets silently dropped out of the Christian understanding of how devotional purity works without getting commented on or even noticed. It's not just about purity, it's about the contingentness and false universality of most of Abrahamic religion's entire apparatus of devotionally constructed emotion. It's like I'm an Asperger's kid at a party with a hired comedian, who has to restrain themselves from explaining the jokes and ruining everyone's experience.

And it's frustrating because I feel like there should be a kind of piety where you can just say stuff like that. The Old Testament God even sometimes exposes a face that seems like it would be compatible with that kind of piety, like when He's blunt and earthy about human failings.

(Really, it's not just Abrahamic religion. There are other glaring points that also pop out to me in the conflicts between the Western occult worldview and the lived worlds of materialists. Maybe I'm just more driven to dig into places where one viewpoint seems to want to call out ways that another viewpoint shouldn't take something for granted, while doing so in the second viewpoint's terms instead of just assuming it's all wrong.)

comments.title: Astrology Book for casting a chart by hand
comments.content:
First, a merry monday to all.

Second, I have watched over the years people asking for suggestions for beginner astrology books and would like to finally make a recommendation, particularly after last week's comment about how someone learning should really learn how to cast a chart by hand before depending on programs.

Back in 1992 I came across a book, Alan Oken's Complete Astrology - the 1988 edition with the black starry cover which was putting 3 books he'd written in '72-'74 from memory - and with it I learned how to cast/calculate a chart by hand using nothing more than pencil and paper, the book, an atlas and the emphemeris at the back of the book. Memory is a bit hazy but i think the first astrolog dos programs were out by then so i could compare and the accuracy was dead on.

Even more amazing was the accuracy of what the sections on houses, planets and aspects had to say when delineating the chart. It had things to say about my own chart/life based on the houses/planets/aspects that were uncanny. A friend asked me to do her chart and I ran into the dilema of do I tell her and maybe put ideas into her head or not and hope for the best - it was a thing that indicated an unwanted and unexpected pregnancy at a particular age. I hummed and hawed and eventually spoke to her and before I could say anything she told me she'd falled pregnant, carried to term and had the child adopted... To this day I don't know whether I should have said anything but done is done.

I've had a small look at the more recent red cover edition easily available and from what I was able to preview it appears the book hasn't been messed with although hard to say without getting a copy, which hasn't been possible - other priorities. So I give it a qualified thumbs up and why I mentioned the 1988 edition which is a definite yes.

The style of the book as well, meant that when I got the Mundane Astrology book with raphael, green etc and alan leo's the key to your own nativity, I found their style very congenial/similar and easy to get to grips with.

I would also like to mention the review someone did of Parker's Astrology - I had also got a copy(the DK edition) but I find it more of a coffee table book to be frank and agreed with the less than flattering review.

Just pulled it from the shelf to give it another look - it does have a section on calculating a chart by hand so can be used thus.

From my own view - whilst it may have the basic information - I found it hard to use it as a reference and not nearly as dead on as the Oken book - it just seems a bit all over the place to me with definite diet and lifestyle and wellness vibes in too many spots.

The illustrations in the dk book are gorgeous to be fair but I consider myself spoiled by Oken book which got down to the nitty gritty and pulled no punches about the meaning and delineation. Perhaps it just seems the Parker book is a bit too fluffy although I may be doing it an injustice. If that's all one can get it can certainly get one started in the very basics. The Oken book is what I would call Old Skool and highly recommend.

All Best,

Parion

replies.title: Re: Book - Thread Spirit
replies.content:
I've kept dithering about whether to mention this, because it's kind of silly, but it's been like the fourth straight week on which someone has brought up the topic of string-related magic and I've dithered whether to bring it up, so maybe I should take that as a sign...

(tl;dr: an explanation of the role and context of string magic in a work of fiction that's currently subculturally very prominent, with a tangent into how the magical worldbuilding makes the fiction very prominent in that subculture, and the relationship of the magical worldbuilding and the string magic to the themes of narrowness, self-constraint, power within the constrained system, and co-developing power outside the constrained system)

There's a Web site for serial-publication fiction called "Royal Road". It has tended to capture the attention of authors and audiences in the genre of "progression" (or, more bluntly, "number goes up") fiction, such as xianxia, LitRPG, and usually isekai, where the protagonist gains power through the sequential acquisition of named techniques and the development of personal attributes or skills through numbered levels or tiered cultivation stages. The currently top-rated ongoing story there is called "Super Supportive", where the very competent author "Sleyca" is making a reconstructive riff on these genres, by trying to worldbuild a more organic origin for the LitRPG genre's obligatory RPG video-game-mechanics-like elements ("the System"), as well as for the isekai genre's summoned-into-another-world elements, and the superpowers of superhero fiction. In the setting of Super Supportive, magic is associated with primordial chaos, and as sapient beings begin to develop the capacity for directed metaphysical self-assertion and so magic, chaos also starts to leak into reality in catastrophically dysregulated forms around them, or potentially within them. (The story faintly hints that when real things try to assert themselves, magical balance requires that other, unreal things living in their shadows should be given the opportunity to assert themselves too.) Sleyca has the System as the face of a complex world-spanning magical construct called the Contract, built by human-like aliens under a treaty with Earth's peoples. The aliens base their Contracts on their existing order-stabilizing magical techniques around contracts and equitable balance, as part of a civilization-wide project to turn the life-threatening chaotic power into a tool for restraining itself. The predefined superpower skills and attributes are a way of channeling and shaping the chaos within (greatly increasing its potency, with the hidden side effect of increasing the potency of the person's unbound power that they could be using for freeform magic). The resulting superpowered humans are a way of controlling and butchering the chaos without. The treaty requires that superpowered humans be available for summoning for reasonable pay, a system initially meant for having a pool of reserve conscripts to fight chaos incursions. However, due to the trading power difference between the worlds caused by the carefully maintained thaumotechnological disparity, in practice most often the summoning is for superpowered industrial, service, or household convenience labor.

The story is set during a time where it's indirectly hinted that magical balance is beginning to demand that humans get access to the free-form magical techniques that have been hoarded by the aliens to keep Earth in the status of a labor "resource world", and so it has begun twisting fate toward that end, with the sympathies and covert support of the Contracts of Earth and of the aliens' homeworld. The path of least resistance for this twisting proved to be the protagonist Alden Thorn. On his first summoning, he ends up stranded with an alien wizard trainee in a remote bunker in the middle of a disaster zone of chaos contamination that's deadly to insufficiently self-assertive living things. She passes along her initial training to Alden as a way of strengthening his self-assertion against the chaos, to help keep them both alive. Trainees use a casting focus called an auriad, which is a loop of specially imbued identity-conductive string. The auriad is formed into various shapes like a cat's cradle between the fingers in sequence. The lengths of the intersecting 1D strand segments, the 2D polygonal faces they construct, and some constructed 3D regions (an internal polyhedron for a crushing spell, or a perpendicular extrusion from a polygonal face for a punching spell) being used as implied channels to help a novice caster's spell organize itself via spatial focus. This help is vital for helping wizard trainees "learn the ropes" of directed self-assertion. The auriads grow personally bonded with use (the trainee's wasn't bonded yet, so she gives it to Alden and later covers for the loss so nobody finds out he's learning wizardry), and physically responsive in minor ways to the possessor's intentions (they don't catch, tangle, or slip if the possessor wishes otherwise). Later, an unconventional alien wizard is seen using a much longer auriad looped as body armor to conduct and focus his physical integrity identity, distributing or cancelling impact forces from hurled objects.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
To invoke, as the word suggests (Latin in-voco, "call in") is to call a spiritual influence into yourself, as when a Christian asks God to send the Holy Spirit to strengthen her.

To evoke (Latin ex-voco, "call out") is to make a spiritual being appear outside yourself so that you can interact with it, as when a mage summons a spirit.

Prayer -- that one's not from Latin! -- is the simple act of speaking to any spiritual being who is omnipresent enough to listen.

replies.title: Hekate
replies.content: Hekate is a Goddess. Pretty straightforward. She was originally very much like Artemis and sometimes assimilated with her, but she is generally a Dark of the Moon Goddess. She is also a psychopomp, having led Demeter to her daughter Persephone in Hades' realm. Funnily, she's a maiden, not a crone. Traditionally Demeter is the Crone, as she disguised herself as such during her search for Persephone. Hekate gets a pretty unfair bad rap these days, being viewed as a dark magic, witchcraft kind of entity, but, actually, in the days of the Neo-Platonists, her role in Theurgy was central. It was only later that she started to be slandered by the christians. It's to bad, as she's one of the (ahem) Kore figures in the Eleusinian story...

replies.title: WG Gray
replies.content:
Alan Richardson's Scattering Light might be worth a look. It's correspondence between Gray and Richardson -- or rather old letters from Gray to Richardson, with commentary and memoir by Richardson from 40 years later.


In January 2023 I found a cache of letters that William G. Gray had written to me 40 years earlier. To my bewilderment, I could remember almost NOTHING about them. Yet they are filled with spiritual observations, deep magickal insights, delicious spleen, endless irresistible yarns and wicked gossip about the ‘witches, bitches and all their little itches’ (his words not mine!) from the 1950s onward, including people who are now Legends. They also included some brilliant ‘unpublished porno’ from Crowley’s Magical Diary dated 1922. What surprised me was that when he wrote these, he was the same age as me. That is, 71½. So I’ve taken the opportunity to reply to these letters as the man I am now: a faux and often phoney Adept; an equal rather than the wide-eyed numpty I was when I first made contact at the age of 17.


It's worth a look for anyone interested in the background to Gray's work.

LeGrand

replies.title: Re: Inner world exploration
replies.content:
This is what I call the Prove-It Game. If you approach occultism in that frame of mind, you will fail, because no matter what you experience, you will always be covertly looking for reasons to insist that it really was just another illusion after all.

My suggestion is that you start by figuring out why you want "this," however defined, to be all there is. You do want that, you know; beliefs, especially beliefs that one feels more and more over time, are the result of desires. Spend some time thinking about the advantages of believing that materialism is true, and come to terms with the desires at the root of that belief. Then you can decide, with a unified will rather than a divided one, what path you want to follow. Just be aware that nobody else is ever going to prove anything to you. You will have to do the work yourself -- and you'll find instructions for doing it, of course, in any of a great many books, mine among them.

replies.title: Re: Inner world exploration
replies.content:
If I may, I was in a similar boat about three years ago. A few years prior to that, I had tried magic as an exercise in archetypes and interaction with my subconscious, but comfortably assumed it was all explainable in a materialistic standpoint. It didn't do much for me, and I dropped it. Along the way, though, I found my attempts to be fully satisfied with materialistic explanations for right, wrong, and meaning ultimately unsatisfying.

What changed things for me was reading our host's A World Full of Gods, which I found convincing enough to open my mind to the possibility that Gods might be real, and then starting a daily practice of ritual, meditation, and divination (in my case, I did the Heathen Lesser Banishing Ritual of the Pentagram and Middle Pillar exercises published elsewhere on this dreamwidth, meditated on the Runes and the Voluspa, and used the Runes for divination). After a while, I had a pretty striking religious experience, and I was like "I guess there's something to this religious stuff after all!" and I've been exploring since.

No idea if that particular path would work for you, but the combination of finding an intellectually satisfying "opening" for possible belief, and then committing to a practice and having my own experience did more for me than a whole lot of reading, thinking, and so forth.

Wherever your search takes you, good luck,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Inner world exploration
replies.content:
If you look at materialism really, really hard, and ask foundational questions like "why this set of impersonal blind physics equations and not that one?" and "what's even going on when we prioritize different possible sets of equations while narrowing things down?" and "why does one set of equations sometimes seem to approximately emerge from another one? metaphorically, if the earth is resting on giant elephants, and the elephants are standing on a giant turtle, what is the turtle standing on, is it turtles all the way down?", you get a substantially different picture, where materiality can occur distilled from mentality and mentality can occur composed from materiality. But maybe it's sometimes importantly virtuous for people to actually experience the process of that questioning all the way through, instead of skipping to the end that I claim one can reach.

I disagree with JMG's pejorative characterization of "that frame of mind", and especially the moral loading connoted by the words with which he characterized the choice of belief or disbelief about the supernatural in terms of "what path you want to follow". I think the supernatural is trying very, very hard to leave open avenues for interpretation of the universe in materialist terms for most people most of the time. And my best guess is that at least a lot of the people who judge that their epistemological duty is to take those avenues are probably not acting in willful bad-faith complicity in knowingly and purposefully choosing that "the path they want to follow" is one of hiding things from themselves, the way his words connote. (The whole point of not knowing whether it's real is that the moral agent doesn't know what would constitute the act of will of knowingly choosing to hide things from themself. Someone can have a sufficiently unclear understanding that they find themselves having to half-consciously choose between "I wouldn't have expected a supernatural that hides behind the appearance of illusion, at best I see an appearance of hiding behind an appearance of illusion, therefore it's all illusion and the supernatural doesn't exist", and "I wouldn't have expected a supernatural that hides behind the appearance of illusion, I think I see an appearance of hiding behind an appearance of illusion, but the supernatural seems to exist and so I guess there isn't a hiding behind an appearance of illusion after all, that's just a talking point materialists use when making the act of will to not examine the evidence"... sorry, I guess I don't have enough time to figure out how to lay out this argument correctly. A complicating factor in criticising JMG's position is that there probably in fact is something that chooses whether to believe or disbelieve, for this one life or some one phase of this life, but it's not something to which the implied moral loading necessarily applies. Materiality itself is partly an illusion, but that doesn't make suspension of disbelief wrong; maybe it's a necessary corrective to another, wider-scoped illusion, like how market discipline is a corrective to what economists call the "resource curse".)

At any rate, we don't know why the supernatural is doing that, and so we should tread cautiously and respectfully around the causal pathways that it seems to have been carefully arranging for, by doing what it's been doing.

replies.title: Re: Inner world exploration
replies.content:
You seem to be reading quite a bit into my words, and much of it is inaccurate. My comment about the Prove-It Game isn't pejorative, it's simply a statement about something I've observed many times in would-be students of occultism, and the results are reliably as I've described them. "Do x, and then y will generally follow" is hardly an insult, you know!

More broadly, you seem to be taking my words a long way out of context. What I've discussed here is the consequence of the internal conflict OP has discussed, between an acknowledged desire to experience an inner world and what, in my experience, is reliably an unacknowledged desire to avoid such experiences. People who have one of those desires and not the other belong to a different conversation -- and yes, many people who embrace materialism uncritically do so because it's the appropriate thing for them to do. OP doesn't appear to be one of those. He's described a conflict; I offered my personal perspective on that conflict -- and to judge by his response, he found that perspective useful.

comments.title: The MacGregors and the Healing Stones of St Fillan
comments.content:
Hi JMG

I was interested to read the other day of your paternal ancestral line coming from the MacGregors as I live in MacGregor country!
I thought you might be interested to hear about the Healing Stones of St Fillan which are kept in a
mill in Killin nearby. They are a set of 8 stones used to heal the parts of the body they represent with a lind of sypathetic magic. Legend has it they were St Fillans healing stones given to the people of Killin in 777AD by the saint apon his death but some heathens would suggest they are older, but dont say that around the church people! They were traditionally kept by a woman in the village related to a milling family. People would knock on her door, cross her palm with silver in exchange, and she would invite them in and rub the correct stone 3 times clockwise, 3 times anticlockwise then 3 times clockwise again on the afflicted part. 2 of the stones have socket holes, and are thought to be pivot stones from an ancient horizontal mill, that the whole mill spun apon. St fillan was also said to have made the first mill on the site so theres a strong milling connection. People both Christian and otherwise visit them and use them to this day and people from the village, particularly the children 'rebed' them on fresh rushes and wash them in river water every christmas eve.

Theres also the Tigh na Cailleach 'plave of the old woman' where river washed stone 'figures' have a all house they are still moved out of every spring and into before Samhain. However thats in Campbell country! You can read more about it here:

https://www.stravaiging.com/history/ancient/site/tigh-na-cailleach/

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

Q1. You have been involved with many magical traditions in your life. You also seem to practice a wide variety of systems to this day.


Druidry is perhaps your main system. Is there anything that the type of Druidry you practice isn't useful for? In other words, is Druidry a suitable stand alone system, or do you need to supplement it with other things?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Druidry is a spiritual philosophy; it's not a system of magic. Most branches of the Druid Revival tradition, in fact, don't teach any operative magic at all. My Druid path underlies my occult work, but the two aren't the same.

In effect, we live in a world of three levels. There's the material level, which is best approached through science and technology, and also through ordinary practical ways of doing things. There's the magical level, which is best approached through occult teachings, meditation, divination, and magic. Then there's the spiritual level, which is best approached through religion, philosophy, contemplation and prayer. Each level is its own thing, and none of them make good substitutes for any of the others.

So on the spiritual level, I'm a Druid. On the magical level, I'm an occultist of what used to be the classic sort in this country a century ago, and I also do some ceremonial magic from time to time. On the material level, I'm a writer (and also a decent cook and some other things). None of these are interchangeable; I don't use incantations to cook hot and sour soup, and I don't use Druid prayer and contemplation to do magical workings!

comments.content:
Hello.

replies.content: This is something I know pretty much nothing about, so I'll have to leave it open to others. Anyone else?

replies.title: Re: Polish/Slavic Magic and Myth and Folk Traditions
replies.content:
One side of my family was Polish-Lithuanian. My grandparents were... well, a little odd. I was young when they died, but I remember their house feeling like a very strange place, especially at night. They had some folk practices, but it's hard to say how much of it came from the old country, and how much was American. They would have both made pretty good Druids.

My grandmother did dream interpretation (we found books and handwritten notes on the subject after she passed). I also remember she took omens by watching birds and weather, and her predictions were often accurate. My sister and I have both had long-gone family members visit us in dreams on occasion - maybe that's related, I'm not sure.

My grandfather was a hunter and fisherman, deeply immersed in nature, who attended Catholic Mass every Sunday. He was also fascinated by Native Americans, and I recall him recounting lore of a local tribe that he must have picked up along the way somehow.

I recall some folk tales of a distinctly Balto-Slavic flavor: the kind that make the hair on your neck and arms stand on end. A marsh in Lithuania that's haunted by ghosts of Mongols, because they tried to invade and the marsh swallowed them instead. A traditional house spirit called a Domovoy, who you must make offerings to daily, with fairly dire consequences if you stop, and they follow you if you move. That kind of thing.

I dont know if that helps or hinders your search.

comments.title: A Very Dramatic TSW Moment
comments.content:
Good evening, Mr. Greer, and commentariat.

No questions this week, but I had a very interesting, and moving experience today that I thought I would share here.
I was walking home from a friend's house this afternoon, and I came across a homeless man overdosing. I wasn't the first one to find him, there was already two city workers there, and the owner of the business he was in front of. The owner of the business called 9-1-1, and one of the city workers was doing chest compressions on the homeless man. I asked some of the shops around if they had OD kits, but they didn't. In any case, I stayed around even though I didn't necessarily have anything to do. It just felt wrong to leave a man who may be dying.
So, I recited the Orphic hymn to Mercury in my head, and prayed that He might aid the homeless man. Then, about a minute later, after the paramedics arrived, and started loading him into a stretcher, he stood up! This man was not breathing two minutes beforehand (he did have a pulse.) but I really must say that I was pretty shocked.
I talked to the man afterwards. His name is John. I hope his life improves.
TSW!
Thanks for hosting this space,

Duncan

replies.title: Re: A Very Dramatic TSW Moment
replies.content: TSW indeed! I'm glad to hear he was okay.

comments.title: Question about imagining yourself to be a mage
comments.content:
JMG:

I finished reading "The Book of Haatan" last week and was struck by a bit in the last chapter.

[SPOILER ALERT}

Grandpa Moravec explains that the most important spell is to imagine yourself as a mage and to will yourself into being a mage. This paragraph got me thinking. I've been working with "Paths of Wisdom" for the last year after finishing up with "Learning Ritual Magic" (which took me almost two years). I still remain skeptical in the efficacy of magic which results from decades of indoctrination into materialism/scientism. And this is happening at the visceral level. At the intellectual level I've left materialism behind but old habits die hard, and I have to catch myself making materialist assumptions and reason them down. This leaves me with two concerns.

(1) Does my lingering skepticism in magic impede my progress? Also, will I hit a ceiling in how far I can get if I am still skeptical, or will continuing to do the work wear down that skepticism? Or is the skepticism necessary in order to stick to the experiences and prevent ideas and models from becoming dogma?

(2) Is the spell of imagining yourself as a mage and willing yourself into being a mage cast by taking up a program like yours and working through it? Or is this another working I should do to address my skepticism in order to make the overall training program progress?

Thank you for your advice.

replies.title: Re: Question about imagining yourself to be a mage
replies.content:
1) It depends on whether you're actually a skeptic, or if what's going in instead is that dogmatic materialism still has its claws into you. Genuine skepticism is a great advantage to the occultist -- but it has to cut both ways; you have to be as skeptical about the claims of science and materialism as you are about those of occultism. If your skepticism points in only one direction it's one mode of inner dishonesty, and will block your progress.

2) Every time you perform a ritual, a meditation, or a divination you are imagining yourself as a mage. You can and should add to that, if you like, by reflection and journaling on themes such as these: what will you do with the capacities that magic is teaching you? How will you weave magical experiences and perceptions into your life? If magic is the art and science in causing changes in consciousness in accordance with will, what changes in your consciousness do you want to bring about? And so on. Explore and keep exploring.

comments.title: Roshomon on Brean Down
comments.content:
Those who have read Dion Fortune's occult novels, especially The Sea Priestess and Moon Magic, might be interested in a recent book by Alan Richardson, who has published quite a bit on Fortune and her various collaborators. I'm going to go into some detail, in case there are people who might be curious.

This book, The Earth Priest, is a re-imagining of the events of the Sea Priestess and Moon Magic, as well as incorporating references to a few other novels as well. Familiar characters are refracted, inverted, split and combined, and characters from Fortune's life and work also make an appearance. For example, David Carstairs has a role as the headmaster of a school for difficult children, and children with difficulties; Fortune and her husband appear as Vi and Penry, who on a walking tour interrupt some mysterious goings-on on Brean Down.

The focal character, the Earth Priest of the title, is a World War I veteran artilleryman, who sets out to reveal the true story behind the events of the two novels. He is also the brother of Vivien; they were both residents of Carstairs' school, where they developed a very difficult relationship.

The Amazon description states –

Wilfred Maxwell’s private diaries were heavily redacted by his ghost-writer ‘Dion Fortune’. When these were finally published as ‘The Sea Priestess’ and marketed as fiction, the book concealed more than it ever revealed about what really happened in that remote Fort in Somersetshire between the Wars. In fact, the heroin addict Maxwell had a lot to hide, and his amanuensis went to very clever lengths to protect him. Here in ‘The Earth Priest’ is a different insight about the events in that remote and haunted place, as told by another participant. And one who reveals a Secret that was hiding in plain sight then - and even more so today, a century later…


Such re-imaginings are not everyone's cup of tea. Anyone who hasn't read the original novels would probably not see how they have been turned inside out and upside down so that their knickers wave in the sun. People who have read the novels, and enjoyed them, might find themselves annoyed by what Richardson has done.

Fortune was given to using unreliable narrators, and Richardson follows her lead even while setting out to give "the other side of the story". He knows a huge amount about the background to the novels, and the people, and his "decoding" of the novels is very clever, and plausible enough.

As far as I know, the book is only available via Amazon, where Richardson has priced it at the lowest possible price ($5.72), which means he receives some two cents per copy sold.

LeGrand

replies.title: Re: Roshomon on Brean Down
replies.content: It sounds very interesting. I'd like to read it, but I'll have to wait until copies show up on the used book market, because I don't support the Big Slimy River and refuse to give it any of my money or data.
